TextENGNew: Late at night, Mom came to find me. She was clutching an envelope in her hand.
"Keiichi... A letter just came for you."
"A letter? For me...?!"
I snatched it from her. It was addressed: "To Kei-chan, from Mion."
What was this, in the middle of the night? Mom said the doorbell had rung, and by the time she reached the door, the envelope had been shoved through the gap.
If she'd gone out of her way to come here, she could have just come inside. ...The fact that she'd avoided that—leaving only a letter—filled me with a vague dread.
But given how abnormal the village had become lately, it made sense that Mion would want to contact us with care.

All the talk of an indigenous virus, or Oyashiro-sama's wrath—it always ended the same way, with suspicious eyes turning toward the outsider families who'd moved in from elsewhere. The entire Maebara household had come to understand that all too painfully in recent days.
Even before opening the letter, I understood that things had reached the point where even Mion—a Sonozaki—couldn't visit us openly in broad daylight.
And yet the contents quickly taught me that even that understanding was still too optimistic.

"To Kei-chan. Be careful! The idea of making an example out of the Maebaras and the Ryuugus is gaining real traction in our council area. I know it's complete insanity, but they've been pushed to the point where they'll be crushed by fear unless they sacrifice someone."
"I could only argue against it in the early days."

"But starting a few days ago, whenever anyone objects, they get told that defending the infected must mean you're infected too. That logic has taken hold, and now no one is stopping the talk of lynching you all!"

"While the virus faction and the Oyashiro-curse faction were equally matched, they'd cancel each other out with their incoherent arguments. But lately, the curse faction has been gaining ground. They're starting to dominate the whole group."
"If Granny were well, she'd never permit anything like this—but she came down with a high fever a few days ago and can't get out of bed."

"Some of my relatives have started spreading rumors that it might be the infection, and my ability to speak at the council is already shaky. I mean—if I'm not careful, they'll come for me as a sacrifice next! In the position I'm in, it's going to be very difficult to speak up on behalf of your household and Rena's anymore."
...I could see Mion's handwriting growing more rushed and distorted as the letter went on. I didn't need to ask how she'd felt while writing it.

"I think it's too dangerous to stay in Hinamizawa any longer. At the very least—if we stay locked in our homes like this, we'll eventually be killed! So I'm planning to escape from the village tomorrow night."
"The SDF has the perimeter sealed, but the Sonozaki family has secret tunnels—and one of them exits outside the quarantine line. Only Granny and I know about these tunnels."
"I wanted to wait until Granny recovered, but it's looking impossible now... She just told me a moment ago to leave her behind and run..."

"...Granny is saying this is a recurrence of the ancient legend—demons pouring out from Onigafuchi Swamp. That everyone in the village is being possessed by demons now, and it will become a bloodbath of villagers killing villagers!"
"In the legends, Oyashiro-sama calms everything eventually—but until that happens, the old stories say there was a bloody, prolonged conflict with countless deaths!"
"They are going to kill us all!"

"That's the main point: tonight, tell your family and have everyone prepare. I'll come for you all tomorrow night, and we'll escape together. Bring cash, your health insurance card, bank book, and personal seal. Fill a backpack with clothes and daily necessities, but don't let it get too heavy. Nothing that needs to be carried by hand!"
"Keep both hands completely free. Wear comfortable shoes you can walk easily in—something like sneakers! I'm sending the same letter to Rena."
changeReason: New translation includes the specific mention of sneakers (スニーカー) from the Japanese, which the old translation omitted.

TextENGNew: "...I know this is coming completely out of nowhere for you, and it probably doesn't feel real yet."
"...But trust me! I've been attending the Three Families' meetings and the council gatherings—and using my radio. I have a very clear sense of just how badly wrong this village has gone!!"
changeReason: New translation includes Mion's use of radio (無線) to gather information, which the old translation omitted entirely.

TextENGNew: "Feel the same urgency I do. Even if you trust me completely and do everything I've asked, I'm estimating a 10% chance at best that you, me, Rena, and our families all make it out of Hinamizawa safely."
That was where Mion's letter ended.
"Wh—...What the hell is this...?!!"
The sheer scale of it was too much to absorb, and I clutched my head. But Mion had foreseen that, and entrusted this letter to me precisely with the words: believe me...!

Three deep breaths later, I could recognize that the danger Mion described wasn't too far removed from reality. The festering air that had settled over Hinamizawa lately made it all too believable. What she had written was the unmistakable truth...!
And at the same time, a cold fear for Satoko took hold.
Rika-chan was in SDF custody—that was one thing. But the letter didn't say a single word about Satoko, who was alone in the village. Only my name and Rena's appeared.
...There was no way Mion had forgotten about her club members. No way she'd accidentally omit Satoko. So why?! Terrible possibilities came at me one after another, each one trying to suffocate me.
That's right—Mion had mentioned it. There were people who genuinely believed that offering Satoko as a sacrifice would appease Oyashiro-sama's wrath.
Could it be... that Satoko had already been...?!
I shot to my feet—and the metal bat leaning against the bookcase toppled over and rolled to my feet.
Almost as though it was asking me to take it along.
...That was when I heard a gunshot.
On any normal, peaceful day, I might have assumed it was some kid setting off firecrackers or bottle rockets. But in a situation this abnormal, I didn't make that mistake.
I opened the curtain and strained to see through the dark—and heard another gunshot. It sounded closer than before. I thought I could make out shouting mixed in with it.
Suddenly, the doorbell began ringing frantically!
Ding dong, ding dong, ding dong, ding dong, ding dong!!
All the noise cleared from my mind. A strange, focused clarity settled over me.
I no longer felt the need to breathe. In that hollow stillness, I picked up the metal bat at my feet and ran for the front door.
Mom was at the entryway, cowering. Dad was running in from the kitchen, carving knife in hand.
...Just moments ago I would have found it odd to see Dad responding so violently. In this specific moment, it was exactly the right response.
Mom looked back at Dad, uncertain whether to open the door. He waved her back.
Someone had pressed the gate buzzer, come through the gate, and was now right at our front door.
Then—BANG BANG BANG BANG BANG BANG—urgent pounding on the door!!
"Wh—Who's there?!"
Dad shouted. An answer came back immediately.
"Good evening—it's Ryuugu! Please open up!"
"It's Rena!!"
I pushed past my father and ran forward. From the sound of Rena's voice, I knew this was an emergency—we had to open the door right now.
Even if the village had descended into a nightmare of suspicion and bloodshed... we club members would never doubt each other!!
"Wh—what?! M-Mion?! Are you all right?!"
"She is absolutely not all right! We need to stop the bleeding now...!"
Rena was carrying Mion on her back—Mion, who was soaked in blood and barely breathing. Asking if she was all right was a completely wasted question.
Rena cut through any further questions, carried Mion in through our door, and laid her on the floor. Mion's left arm was badly injured, her clothes dyed red.
changeReason: Old translation says 'left shoulder' but JPN 左腕 means 'left arm'; new translation corrects this anatomical detail.

TextENGNew: ...Her clothes were supposed to be yellow.
The shirt Mion always wore in her free time, that bright cheerful yellow she put on once school let out—the shirt that always made me feel like a different, better kind of time was about to start—
Was drenched and obliterated in scarlet.
"M-Mom!! She needs help! Bring a towel—something, anything...!!"
changeReason: Old translation renders 母さん (Mom) as 'Hey', losing that Keiichi is directly calling out to his mother for help.

TextENGNew: Mion was pressing her wound with a handkerchief, but the blood kept coming.
...If you'd wrung it out, it would have dripped. Whatever color it had been, whatever pattern, whether it had been Mion's favorite—none of that was discernible anymore.
"Wh-What on earth happened...?! Rena-chan, what is this...?!"
Dad asked, still not putting down his carving knife.
Rena's face twisted as she tried to figure out where to begin. Her expression said it clearly: we don't have time for explanations.
...But perhaps none was needed. The bright red blood saturating her own clothes, from carrying Mion, told the story better than any words could.
And that explanation came more quickly than any way Rena could have imagined explaining it.
changeReason: Old translation adds 'moments later we heard a tremendous bang outside our front door' which is not in the Japanese text for this line; new translation correctly limits to the actual content of this entry.

TextENGNew: A gunshot hole had appeared somewhere in the house.
changeReason: Old asks 'Did she have a gunshot wound?' but JPN 銃痕ができた？ means 'A bullet hole appeared?' referring to the house being shot; new translation corrects this.

TextENGNew: The realization hit within a single heartbeat. She was shot...!! Someone had fired at our front door!!!
"Uwaaaaaah!!!"
Rena spun at the sound of the shot—swinging the large hatchet she'd been carrying in her free hand, even while bearing Mion's weight.
"U—Uwaaaaaah!!!"
"K-Keiichi?!"
I gripped my bat and charged after Rena barefoot. I didn't even hear my father calling behind me.
"Re—Renaaaa!!!"
Rena leapt on the figure standing at our front gate and brought the hatchet down without mercy.
changeReason: Old adds 'splitting his head open' which is not in the Japanese (容赦なく振り下ろした means 'brought down without mercy'); new translation is more faithful to the source.

TextENGNew: The moment was incomprehensible and over in an instant.
Rena turned to a second man, swung the heavy hatchet easily overhead, and hurled it at him with shattering force!
It flashed silver in the moonlight and buried itself in the man's shoulder with a dark spray.
changeReason: Old says the hatchet hit 'the man's face' but JPN 肩口 means 'shoulder area'; new correctly translates as 'shoulder' and includes the dark spray detail from the Japanese.

TextENGNew: The man let out a short scream.
changeReason: Old adds 'as a small, dark spray spewed from his head' which is not in this line's Japanese text (it belonged to the previous entry); new correctly limits to just the scream.

TextENGNew: My mind was split between normal social morality screaming what are you doing, Rena!—and the question of what possible threat could have warranted a response like this.
Rena walked to the man who'd staggered with her hatchet in his shoulder, wrenched it free, and raised the heavy blade overhead.
changeReason: Old says 'hatchet in his face' and 'slammed into his head over and over' but JPN describes the hatchet in his shoulder (肩口) and only raising it overhead (振り上げる); new correctly translates both details.

TextENGNew: The man begged her to stop, over and over. Rena paid no attention to him.
She brought it down in a single stroke. Then she confirmed he wasn't moving, and spoke.
changeReason: New includes the striking action '一息に振り下ろす' (brought it down in a single stroke) which the old translation omitted entirely.

TextENGNew: "...Ki...Killing someone means being prepared to be killed yourself! Don't think you can do it cheaply!!"
It was almost as though those words were aimed at me.
...From Rena's perspective, my face must have read as the blank look of someone still too accustomed to peace to understand what was happening.
And then Rena spun around and shouted—
"That man's still moving!!"
changeReason: Old says 'still alive' but JPN まだ動いてる means 'still moving'; in combat context this distinction matters as 'moving' implies ongoing threat, not just being alive.

TextENGNew: The man Rena had knocked down first—the one who'd fired at our door—was clutching his forehead and trying to stand.
Surely he'd been hurt enough by now that we could let it go... But that thought was just my peace-bred instinct talking.

...Rena didn't think that at all. One hand was pressed to his forehead, but the other still held his hunting rifle. As long as he had the strength to pull a trigger, his threat wasn't neutralized...!!
"Uraaaaaaaaah!!!"
The thick mass of iron swung down on him once more.
This time, the man stopped moving.
changeReason: Old says 'the man's head cracked open' but JPN 男の活動を停止させる means 'stopped the man's activity/movement'; new translation is faithful to the source text.

TextENGNew: Rena turned back and glared—right at me. ...I had been the one closest to that man, not Rena. She'd shouted so I would take him down. But I stood there drifting, and Rena had to do it herself.
He'd had a gun. If he'd been faster, Rena couldn't have reached him from where she stood. That's why she'd trusted me with him—and I stood there, frozen, still unable to make sense of things...
Her expression made all of that clear, and I apologized before I could think.
"It's fine! Just hurry!"
Rena yanked up the hatchet and picked up the hunting rifle with her other hand.
changeReason: Old says 'yanked her hatchet out of the man's head' but JPN 鉈を持ち上げ simply means 'lifted the hatchet'; new removes the added graphic detail.

TextENGNew: "Even if you can't fire it, a gun can still intimidate people."
"Ah—...R-Right..."
"More importantly, get ready to run right now!!"
...Wait, didn't Mion's letter say tomorrow...?
I needed to stop clinging to that false sense of normalcy. Now.
"Oh—...Yeah!! But tell me—what happened?!"
I asked as we ran back toward the front door.
"...You got a letter too, right, Keiichi-kun? Mii-chan brought the same letter to my house in person."
"That's when the neighborhood watch found her and shot her! Mii-chan hadn't done anything. She was only delivering a letter to me, and they shot her...!"

"...They decided all on their own that the Ryuugus were infected, and that anyone in contact with us was an ally of the infected—and then they just shot her for it!! Mii-chan hadn't done a single thing to them, and they didn't care!!"
Rena's eyes were full of grief and fury, tears spilling down her face. There were no words to give her in that moment...
When we got back inside, Mion had been given a towel, and Mom was with her, helping hold pressure on the wound.
Mom was barely holding herself together. ...The most we had in the house was band-aids, headache medicine, cold remedies. There was nothing here to treat a gunshot wound.
Rena returned inside with fresh blood splattered on her clothes and coating the hatchet. Given what had just happened and the hunting rifle in her hands, Dad seemed to understand everything.
...Rena saw that he understood, and spoke.
"Sir—if we don't leave this place right now, we'll be killed. ...My father has already been murdered!! Just now! He tried to save Mii-chan... and they didn't hesitate for a second!"
"They shot him as easily as if they were driving off crows!! I can't believe it... I can't believe it!!!"
At that cry, Mion's lips parted faintly...
"...........I'm sorry...... Rena... ......If I hadn't slipped up......... ......I'm really...... sorry......"
"...It isn't your fault, Mii-chan. And I'm not blaming anyone anymore. Everyone who deserved to be held accountable—they've been settled with. Including the one who shot you, Mii-chan."
...Just minutes ago, Rena had watched Mion get shot and her father get killed, with her own life in the balance—and she'd clawed her way out of that hell on her own.
The expression on her face now was one I had never once glimpsed in our ordinary lives—and it made me understand the depth of what she'd been through, without her having to say a word.
"...Anyway... we need to run, now... They'll be here any minute...!"
Why was the mob coming here? What were they going to do? The only one I wouldn't have needed to ask those questions was Mion—and she was lying on the floor bleeding.
So Dad swallowed his confusion, cleared his throat, and asked just one thing.
"R-Run... where to?!"
"There's a hidden tunnel in the secret basement of Mii-chan's house—no one in the village knows about it! If we can make it there, we might be all right...!"
Rena explained on Mion's behalf—the same thing that had been written in the letter. Mion had tried to take over the explanation, but choked instead.
"Dad, Mom—w-we don't have a moment to spare...! Let's go!"
"...R-Right. Let's go!"
Dad needed a few seconds to register the urgency and set aside the morality of his ordinary life—but he nodded. ...Mom, on the other hand, couldn't move.
"B-But! If we run, what happens to our house?! We don't know what's going to happen!"
If the mob came here, they might well burn the house down. ...But if Mom understood that much, her hesitation was contradictory.
If they were willing to burn the house down with us inside, what good would holding out here do? What would they do to us then...?

...Mom wasn't foolish. Maybe that was precisely why her thoughts were spinning too fast to be useful. In this specific moment, Dad—who had been on edge since the first day of the quarantine—was actually the steadier one.
Mom managed to regain enough composure to go back for her handbag with the bank book.
Dad took the hunting rifle from Rena.
...He boasted that he knew his way around one, having handled a similar type of air gun before. Whether that was reassuring or not probably depended on who was listening.
Mion insisted we leave her behind—that she'd only slow us down. Rena slapped her hard across the face for suggesting it.
"No! Without you, we won't know where the secret basement is! Don't throw your responsibility away so lightly!! Now lead us there!!"
"...Ha ha......... With you around... you won't even let me die easy..."
Mion at least had enough fight left to talk back. That was something.
But we couldn't leave her wound unattended indefinitely. Without proper medical attention, the blood loss alone would eventually become fatal.
"...Hey, Mii-chan. .........We don't have time to argue, so just answer me straight."
Rena fixed Mion with a dry, unflinching look and asked her one thing.
"Why were you suggesting we escape without Satoko-chan?"
"...Th-That's what I wanted to ask too!! What happened with Satoko, Mion?! She's in a worse position than any of us, isn't she?! We can't just leave Satoko behind!!"
Mion trailed off. Rena wasn't letting her avoid the question.
"If she's dead, then say so...!! Because if you don't, I'm going to believe she's still alive!!"
Without thinking I squeezed my eyes shut... That worst-case possibility had crossed my mind more than once.
...There was no way Mion would ever forget about her club members. No way she'd leave Satoko out by accident.
That left only one reason... I had thought of it too. But I couldn't say it out loud...!!
"......Sa..... Satoko's been... taken by the mob, apparently... They say they dragged her to Onigafuchi... Following the old sacrifice ritual—they're going to take three days to drown her..."
"She's still alive?!" / "So she's still alive?!"
Rena and I both seized on that at the same moment.
Neither of us had the slightest thought of blaming Mion for nearly leaving Satoko behind.
...Mion had weighed the information she'd gathered as a Sonozaki, analyzed it coolly, and concluded—through agonized deliberation—that there was no way to save her!
"...There's nothing I can do...! I couldn't do anything...!! Ngh!"

"The council leaders are spearheading it—the shrine group, the youth group, the neighborhood watch, with villager volunteers filling out the numbers to over a hundred people!! The hunting club has joined the watch, so they're armed with at least twenty shotguns and rifles!"
"Everyone is chanting Oyashiro-sama's name like they're possessed!! There's no mercy left in them—they shot villagers who tried to protect Satoko without the slightest hesitation!!"
changeReason: Old renders the line as hypothetical ('If I tried to protect Satoko'), but JPN describes what already happened: villagers who actually tried to protect Satoko were shot; new translation corrects this.

TextENGNew: "Like they did to you and to my father...?"

Mion nodded, bitter tears streaming down her face. She wept at her own powerlessness—at not being able to stop any of it—and for the villagers who had lost their lives trying to hold onto what was right.
"I can't understand it!! Three days ago, everyone was still normal—still rational!! In just these 72 hours they've turned completely. Their eyes are wild, and they've lost all reason!"
"It wasn't the SDF leaving that made them like this! They were already going wrong before that!! The killer virus isn't what's terrifying anymore—it's the people of this village...!!"
"............I... keep thinking about it. .........This virus... it doesn't kill people—it drives people to madness, don't you think?"
changeReason: Old translation includes content from the next entry ('making the villagers become demons one after another'); new correctly limits this entry to its own Japanese text about the virus driving madness rather than death.

TextENGNew: "......And it turns the villagers into demons, one after another... retracing the legend of Oyashiro-sama all over again...!"
changeReason: Old omits the key detail about 'turning villagers into demons one after another' (村人を次々、鬼に変えていって) which was erroneously placed in the previous entry; new translation includes the complete content.

TextENGNew: "Only Oyashiro-sama can resolve this! And her reincarnation—Rika-chan—is gone!"
"And in the first place, Oyashiro-sama was a god—not a human! There is nothing we humans can do. Nothing!!!"
"...Calm down, Mion...!"
I crouched beside her and—knowing it would hurt her wound—pulled her head firmly against me and let her hear my heartbeat...

"...............Thank you. ...While Rena and I had no idea what was happening and were just drifting along, you were working hard in the shadows of this village, fighting to hold back the rumors... ......That's surely why the village didn't fall into madness until today..."
"......If you hadn't been fighting... the terrible day we're facing now might have swallowed us all long before this."
"...............So thank you, Mion."
